text: Welcome to The Long View. Today we're exploring solar energy in depth with Dr. Elena Fischer, energy systems researcher.
Thanks, Alex Morgan. This is a topic I've spent decades on.

This episode is brought to you by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first, fast search, and a clean roadmap view your team actually uses. Get started at linear.com/podcast.
Why is reliability the part that most analyses get wrong?

I'd put it this way: Solar's learning curve was the most important industrial story of the last 40 years. Teams working with NREL run into this constantly. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
That maps to what I've seen.

That ties to another point — Most solar discourse confuses LCOE with delivered cost — they diverge sharply in low-irradiance regions. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
What's the deepest piece of context on sustainability that listeners are missing?

There are two angles — Storage is solving slower than generation but the curve is real — battery costs are tracking the solar trajectory. Teams working with BloombergNEF run into this constantly. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Is there a counterexample that would change your view?

That ties to another point — Sustainability claims around solar are accurate over the lifecycle, but only if you do the manufacturing footprint honestly. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Walk me through how reliability interacts with the broader solar energy story.

Let me give you the version I actually believe. Grid reliability with high renewables is a systems-thinking problem, not a technology one. Teams working with IEA run into this constantly. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Is there a counterexample that would change your view?

That ties to another point — I changed my mind on rooftop solar. I used to think utility-scale was strictly better. Distributed has resilience properties I underweighted. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
What's the deepest piece of context on reliability that listeners are missing?

There are two angles — Most solar discourse confuses LCOE with delivered cost — they diverge sharply in low-irradiance regions. Teams working with IPCC run into this constantly. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
That maps to what I've seen.
That ties to another point — Solar's learning curve was the most important industrial story of the last 40 years. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
